skill.name: react-native-architecture
skill.description: Build production React Native apps with Expo, navigation, native modules, offline sync, and cross-platform patterns.
skill.summary: Design Expo-based React Native apps with navigation, native integrations, offline data, performance, and release patterns.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged command execution, network links, credential JSON, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found a Markdown-only React Native guidance skill. The shell commands are documented npx and EAS CLI examples, the URLs are official documentation links, and the credential path is a placeholder with no embedded secret. No prompt injection, hidden execution, credential exfiltration, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: User-Run CLI Commands in Documentation
low_findings.description: The external command alerts are mostly Markdown fence false positives. The real commands are documented npx and EAS CLI examples that require user action and are normal for React Native setup, but users should review third-party package and build commands before running them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are fenced documentation examples and do not execute automatically. They invoke standard Expo and EAS tooling, so the residual risk is user-run supply-chain exposure rather than malicious skill behavior.

low_findings.title: External Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL alerts point to Expo, React Native, and FlashList documentation resources. No code sends data to these URLs, and no suspicious tracking or exfiltration endpoint was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The links are visible resource references to reputable project documentation. There is no network execution path or data transfer logic in the skill file.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Service Account Path
low_findings.description: The credential JSON alert refers to a sample EAS submit configuration with serviceAccountKeyPath set to ./google-services.json. No credential material is embedded, but production users should keep service account files out of source control.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The finding is a path string inside an example configuration, not a secret value. It still touches credential handling, so low residual risk remains if users copy the pattern without secret management controls.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Alerts Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alert at the description line and reconnaissance alerts in React Native examples are not supported by the surrounding content. The reviewed text contains architecture guidance, navigation parameters, platform checks, and performance examples, not cryptographic code or host reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found ordinary prose, typed route parameters, UI callbacks, and EAS commands. No weak cryptographic algorithm or system reconnaissance behavior is present.

content.user_title: Architect React Native Apps with Expo
content.value_statement: React Native teams need repeatable patterns for navigation, native APIs, offline data, and releases. This skill provides Expo-focused architecture guidance and examples that help Claude, Codex, and Claude Code shape production mobile apps.
actual_capabilities: Explains Expo project structure and when Expo or bare React Native fits best.
Provides Expo Router navigation patterns for tabs, dynamic routes, and programmatic routing.
Shows authentication flow patterns using Expo SecureStore and protected route groups.
Covers offline-first data handling with React Query persistence and network status awareness.
Demonstrates native integrations for haptics, biometrics, push notifications, and platform-specific UI.
Includes EAS build, submit, OTA update, and performance optimization guidance.
limitations: It provides architecture guidance and examples, not a complete generated application.
Examples assume Expo, TypeScript, and modern React Native tooling.
Placeholder API, model, and style names must be adapted to each project.
Production credentials, app store setup, and platform testing still need separate review.

use_cases.title: Plan a New Mobile App
use_cases.description: Create a maintainable Expo project structure with routing, providers, services, hooks, and feature components.

use_cases.title: Add Production App Patterns
use_cases.description: Introduce authentication, offline data caching, native device APIs, and release workflows into an existing app.

use_cases.title: Review React Native Architecture
use_cases.description: Compare a current implementation against Expo, navigation, state, performance, and platform-specific best practices.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Starter Architecture
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new Expo project.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the react-native-architecture skill to design a starter Expo app structure for a TypeScript mobile app. Include folders, providers, navigation groups, and the first implementation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Design Navigation and Auth
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the react-native-architecture skill to plan Expo Router navigation with authenticated and unauthenticated areas. Include protected route behavior and token storage guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Add Offline Data Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the react-native-architecture skill to design offline-first product data flows with React Query persistence, optimistic updates, and network reconnection behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Production App Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the react-native-architecture skill to review my React Native release plan. Check navigation, native modules, performance, EAS build profiles, app store submission, and platform risks.

output_examples.input: Plan the architecture for a new Expo marketplace app.
output_examples.output: A concise architecture outline with app folders, route groups, shared providers, feature modules, service boundaries, and phased setup steps.

output_examples.input: Help me add offline support to product browsing and creation.
output_examples.output: A data-flow plan covering cached queries, persisted storage, optimistic creation, retry behavior, and conflict-handling decisions.

output_examples.input: Review my mobile release checklist before app store submission.
output_examples.output: A release readiness review covering EAS profiles, native permissions, credentials, OTA update strategy, device testing, and performance checks.

best_practices: Share current app structure, Expo SDK version, navigation approach, and native module requirements before asking for changes.
Ask for platform-specific notes when a feature behaves differently on iOS, Android, or web.
Review and adapt every CLI command, credential path, permission, and app store setting before using it in production.
anti_patterns: Do not copy placeholder app IDs, email addresses, credentials, or API names into production configuration.
Do not treat the examples as complete security, error handling, or compliance implementations.
Do not run package installation or EAS commands without checking versions, project state, and repository policy.

faq.question: Does this skill work best with Expo or bare React Native?
faq.answer: It is centered on Expo and EAS workflows, while still explaining when bare React Native may be needed.

faq.question: Can it generate a full mobile app?
faq.answer: It can guide structure and implementation patterns, but project-specific code, assets, APIs, and tests are still required.

faq.question: Does it cover authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a protected route pattern and token storage approach using Expo SecureStore.

faq.question: Does it support offline-first apps?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers React Query persistence, network status tracking, stale data, and optimistic updates.

faq.question: Are the build commands safe to run?
faq.answer: They are standard Expo and EAS examples, but users should verify package versions, credentials, and project settings first.

faq.question: What should I provide for the best result?
faq.answer: Provide your Expo version, routing plan, target platforms, native modules, API shape, and release requirements.
